title: Mercer Super agrees to pay $10.3m fine to ASIC
full_text:
One of Australia’s biggest superannuation funds, Mercer, has agreed to pay a $10.3m fine to the corporate cop after conceding it had failed to tell it about internal investigations into serious member services issues.
Mercer will also pay legal costs to the Australian Securities and Investments Commission, worth $1.2m, the Federal Court heard on Wednesday. The court has not yet delivered its judgment on the case, but both sides have agreed on the penalties.
ASIC alleged that between 2021 and 2024, Mercer failed to report seven internal investigations to it, as required by law. The investigations were launched after insurance premiums were not being refunded correctly after members had died, after member accounts were not created with default insurance, and when member information was not updated by the trustee.
ASIC also alleged that Mercer Super provided false or misleading information in reports to ASIC, which understated the number of members impacted.
Mercer Super barrister Nick de Young, KC, told the court the superfund had co-operated with ASIC and sought to resolve the matter quickly. He said Mercer did not deliberately engage in the conduct and had since invested in “significant” improvements to its compliance systems. Judge Catherine Button will decide if she agrees with the proposed penalties, and deliver her judgment at a future hearing.
